summary: Jeff Atwood proposes an unconventional solution to the problem of link rot: instead of using traditional absolute URLs that break over time, build links using Google search terms with the 'I'm Feeling Lucky' parameter. By extracting 3-4 unique words from a target page and constructing a Google query, you create links that remain valid even when content moves to new locations. He acknowledges the obvious downside—making Google the 'link DNS' for the entire internet—but argues it's a practical workaround for preserving access to referenced content.
key_points: Dead links are inevitable as web pages age, making absolute URLs inherently fragile
Google's 'I'm Feeling Lucky' search can serve as a permanent redirect to content, even when URLs change
The approach requires extracting 3-4 unique identifying words from the target page
Widespread adoption would make Google dangerously powerful as the internet's 'link DNS'
The technique is practical for individual use even if not suitable for universal adoption
money_quotes: Why not build all of our links using relative Google search terms?
Now you have a permanent, unbreakable link to that content.
Google would become unbelievably powerful. They would be the 'link DNS' for the entire internet.

Whenever I link to articles in my code, I try to do so through very specific google search terms, which are likely to produce valid links many years from now – even if the content moves to a different place on the internet.
key_insight: Search engines can function as a content-addressable layer for the web, making links resilient to URL changes by identifying content through its substance rather than its location.
